attribution: Transcript data: TalkMoves dataset (SumnerLab / Suresh et al.), CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, speaker-blinded before any model call.
caveats: The calculator checks a claim only when it can ground it; 'not checked' means outside its current coverage, not wrong.
Reading a speaker's move is interpretive. The model proposes the reading; only the claim verdicts are computed.
One automated read of one transcript — a starting point for your own reading, not a verdict on the discussion.

transcript:
# tm_0083

U0001 S01: Ok. Graham and Kelly had something very interesting to say  about why um…one half should go in the same, is another  name for two fourths and should go in the same place.
U0002 S01: Graham do you want uh to tell us?
U0003 S01: Graham told me.
U0004 S02: Well, one half plus one half equals a whole, and two fourths  plus two fourths equals a whole.
U0005 S01: Well that's an interesting argument one half plus one half  equals a whole, and two fourths plus two fourths equals a  whole.
U0006 S01: Does that make sense?
U0007 S03: Uh I think they're kind of off.
U0008 S03: It's true, but they're kind of  off.
U0009 S03: It's true that one half plus one half equals one whole, but  two fourths plus two fourths equals four fourths which is one  whole.
U0010 S01: [Writing on the chalk board] OK, so we have one half plus  one half equals one whole, two fourths plus two fourths  equals one whole, or two fourths plus two fourths equals  four fourths equals one whole.
U0011 S01: How many of you agree with that?
U0012 S01: Does that make sense?
U0013 S01: That's very neat.
U0014 S01: I hope you notice, Mrs. Phillips, that  they're adding fractions.
U0015 S04: Yes, I noticed.
U0016 S01: That's very neat.
U0017 S01: That's very neat.
U0018 S01: Ok.
U0019 S01: That's very neat.
U0020 S01: David?
U0021 S01: Hold on let's hear what David has to say for a  moment?
U0022 S05: Well I was thinking.
U0023 S05: That uh that like four fourths equals  one half which equals two halves.
U0024 S01: Say that one more time David
U0025 S05: Uh, four fourths should be equals one half [T writes on  the board]
U0026 S03: four fourths equals one half ?
U0027 S03: four fourths?
U0028 S03: Two fourths.
U0029 S05: two fourths….oh wait one whole
U0030 S03: four fourths is equal to one whole
U0031 S05: Yeah that's what I mean.
U0032 S01: You want me to change this?
U0033 S01: four fourths equals one whole
U0034 S05: Yeah um and two fourths is equal to one half
U0035 S01: Why?
U0036 S05: Because um….two fourths uh would be equal to right up  right next to is like in the middle of like one whole and um
U0037 S01: In the middle between numbers?
U0038 S05: zero and one.
U0039 S01: Between zero and one.
U0040 S01: Ok.
U0041 S05: and um so then one half of it would be in the same place.
U0042 S01: Ok
U0043 S05: So the half would have to be in the same place
U0044 S01: Ok
U0045 S05: because if you put them right next to each other the one half  would be in the same place as both of them because would  one half would be equals on both sides
U0046 S04: What might be helpful is that David drew a picture here  which I found very helpful to me in understanding his  argument and I think it might be hard for everyone out there  to understand what David says.
U0047 S01: Would you sketch it up here David?
U0048 S04: Would you please David?
U0049 S04: I think that might help.
U0050 S04: Want to take you picture with you?
U0051 S05: [Sketching using rods on board]…I just drew it like that  because…
U0052 S05: that's the one whole, um these are the one fourth, and that's  the one half, and this would be zero and that would be one.
U0053 S01: Let me ask you to do something here that might help me.
U0054 S01: I  want these to be here.
U0055 S01: Can you place your numbers here  now.
U0056 S01: [T draws in lines to turn the rods into a number  line.]
U0057 S05: You mean like one half .
U0058 S01: Where zero go underneath, no underneath like the number  line.
U0059 S01: [David draws in zero,1/4,two fourths, three fourths,  one, one half on the number line.]
U0060 S01: Ok so what I'm imagining when you do that David, I'm  imagining the rods and I'm also imagining the number line.
U0061 S01: That's very helpful to me.
U0062 S01: Is that helpful to you what he's  done?
U0063 S01: [SS murmurs ‘yes'] How many of you understand  what David has done?
U0064 S01: Raise you hand if you understand it.
U0065 S01: If not, if you have a question David.
U0066 S01: I'm sure will be happy  to answer it.
U0067 S01: Does anyone have a question for David?
U0068 S01: Now what David is  suggesting which um I think helps me a lot, I don't know if  it helps you, that if you went to place numbers between zero  and one imagining the rods, right?
U0069 S01: It helps you to place those  numbers.
U0070 S06: Yes
U0071 S01: Now once you place the numbers and then once you imagine  the rods it seems to be when the rods would end.
U0072 S01: Right,  where the one half rod ends, is where you would place one  half where it ended here he placed a one.
U0073 S01: Right?
U0074 S01: That's a  very very nice notation.
U0075 S01: I like that a lot.
U0076 S01: What do the rest of  you think of that?
U0077 S07: I like it.
U0078 S01: [To Alan]  What do you think?
U0079 S01: Thank you very much David
U0080 S08: I agree with him.
U0081 S01: Isn't that nice.
U0082 S01: That's very nice.
U0083 S01: How many of you like that?
U0084 S01: Yeah, I like that a lot.
U0085 S01: Maybe we can adapt that as an  interesting notation.
U0086 S01: If we were inventing our own notation  that would be a very useful one.
U0087 S01: Jakki?
U0088 S09: It's sort of like the Cuisenaire Rods.
U0089 S01: Yeah that helps me a lot doesn't it?
U0090 S10: It's supposed to be the purple rod, one fourth would be the  white and the half is like the red, or the purple I think.
U0091 S01: Jessica?
U0092 S11: Well, I think it is sort of a new way to make a number line.
U0093 S01: It's a way to build it isn't it?
U0094 S11: Yeah.
U0095 S01: That's a neat idea.
U0096 S01: Yeah, I think so, too.
U0097 S01: Michael?
U0098 S12: Uhm, I, when I was working at home trying to make a  number line I um found out that if you um do like one whole  divided by two you would need would get like one half  because you would take one plus….like that….and then or  you could get two fourths if you divided it by two so that  would prove that two fourths and one half are the same.
U0099 S01: That's very nice.
U0100 S01: I think that would prove it to me, would it  prove it to the rest of you?
U0101 S01: James?
U0102 S13: Uhm, I have another way to do it.
U0103 S13: I also drew a picture.
U0104 S01: You did?
U0105 S01: Ok, do you like to share it with us?
U0106 S13: Ok
U0107 S01: Ok.
U0108 S01: Sure.
U0109 S01: Would you like to do it on this side?
U0110 S13: Ok.
U0111 S13: This is one half [refers to right circle] and this is two  fourths [referring to left circle], two fourths I made a circle  and divided it into 4ths and colored in two fourths and it  shows it equals one half.
U0112 S13: One side of this and two sides of  this both are one half.
U0113 S01: What do you think of that?
U0114 S07: s I agree.
U0115 S01: What do you think of that?
U0116 S01: How many of you understand  what James did here?
U0117 S01: We had another way of trying to  justify that one half was the same as two fourths.
U0118 S01: And that  would therefore enable you to put them in the same place on  the number line?
U0119 S13: Yes
U0120 S01: Thank you very much James.
U0121 S01: Uh hah!
U0122 S01: Interesting.
U0123 S01: Jakki.
